faq.a: Most factory remote start systems are only available on automatic transmission vehicles due to safety concerns. Some aftermarket systems support manual transmissions with additional safety procedures.

title: Driver Seat Belt Buckle Switch Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: The driver seat belt buckle switch circuit has an electrical fault.
fullDescription:
Code B1500 indicates a fault in the driver seat belt buckle switch circuit. The seat belt buckle switch detects whether the driver's seat belt is fastened and communicates this information to the BCM and airbag module. This data is used for the seat belt warning chime, seat belt reminder light, and airbag deployment strategy.

A faulty buckle switch can cause the seat belt warning chime to sound continuously even when the belt is fastened, or it may not sound at all when the belt is unfastened. More critically, the airbag module uses seat belt status to adjust airbag deployment force.

The buckle switch is integrated into the seat belt buckle assembly. It uses either a mechanical contact switch or a Hall effect sensor that detects the metal seat belt tongue insertion.
safetyImpact: Airbag deployment strategy may not be optimized for seat belt status. Seat belt warning system compromised.

causes.cause: Debris in seat belt buckle preventing full insertion

symptoms: Seat belt warning chime sounds continuously
Seat belt warning light stays on even when buckled
No seat belt warning when belt is unfastened
Airbag warning light may also illuminate

fixSteps.title: Check for debris
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the seat belt buckle for coins, crumbs, or other debris that may prevent the tongue from fully inserting and engaging the switch.

fixSteps.title: Test buckle switch
fixSteps.instruction: Use a multimeter to check the buckle switch continuity. It should change state when the belt is buckled and unbuckled.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the wiring from the buckle to the under-seat connector. Objects under the seat can pinch or cut these wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace buckle assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the seat belt buckle assembly. The switch is integrated and not separately replaceable on most vehicles.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with this code?
faq.a: You can drive, but the airbag system may not know your seat belt status, which can affect deployment force. Additionally, you may have no seat belt reminder if the switch is stuck in the buckled position.

faq.q: Can I clean the seat belt buckle to fix this?
faq.a: Sometimes. Debris in the buckle is a common cause. Use compressed air to blow out the buckle slot. Avoid using lubricants as they can attract more debris.

title: Passenger Seat Occupant Detection Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The front passenger seat occupant detection sensor has a circuit fault.
fullDescription:
Code B1510 indicates a fault in the passenger seat occupant detection system. This system determines whether the front passenger seat is occupied and, on many vehicles, estimates the occupant's size and weight to optimize airbag deployment.

The occupant detection sensor uses pressure sensors, weight sensors, or capacitive sensing embedded in the seat cushion. The data is sent to the airbag module to determine whether to deploy the passenger airbag and at what force level. Small children and infant car seats should suppress airbag deployment.

A fault in this system may cause the passenger airbag to deploy at full force for a small occupant or suppress deployment when a full-size adult is seated. Both conditions are dangerous and this code should be addressed promptly.
safetyImpact: Passenger airbag deployment strategy compromised. Airbag may deploy inappropriately for occupant size.

causes.cause: Seat foam compression affecting sensor readings

symptoms: Passenger airbag off indicator lit when adult is seated
Passenger airbag light not illuminating for empty seat

fixSteps.title: Verify condition
fixSteps.instruction: Note the passenger airbag status indicator with different weight objects on the seat. An adult should show airbag ON, an empty seat should show airbag OFF.

fixSteps.title: Check under-seat wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the wiring harness under the passenger seat for damage. Objects stored under the seat can pinch or sever these wires.

fixSteps.title: Scan for detailed codes
fixSteps.instruction: Use an SRS-capable scan tool to read specific fault details about the occupant sensor circuit (open, short, out of range).

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor mat
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor mat has failed, the seat cushion cover must be removed to access and replace it. This is a labor-intensive procedure requiring careful reassembly.

faq.q: Why does my passenger airbag light say OFF when someone is sitting there?
faq.a: The occupant detection sensor may be reading the occupant as too light (below the threshold for airbag deployment). This can be caused by a faulty sensor or a lightweight passenger.

faq.q: Can aftermarket seat covers affect the occupant sensor?
faq.a: Yes. Thick seat covers can prevent the pressure or capacitive sensor from accurately detecting the occupant. This can cause false readings and trigger this code.

title: Rear Parking Assist Sensor Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: A rear parking assist sensor circuit has an electrical fault.
fullDescription:
Code B1600 indicates a fault in the rear parking assist system. This system uses ultrasonic sensors mounted in the rear bumper to detect objects behind the vehicle during reverse operation. The sensors emit ultrasonic pulses and measure the echo return time to calculate distance to objects.

Parking assist sensor failures are commonly caused by physical damage (curb impacts), paint overspray (aftermarket painting), dirt/ice buildup on the sensor face, or water intrusion into the sensor housing. The parking assist module monitors each sensor and sets this code when one is not responding correctly.

While parking assist is a convenience feature, many drivers rely on it extensively, especially in vehicles with limited rear visibility. The backup camera (if equipped) is a separate system.
safetyImpact: Reduced awareness of objects behind the vehicle during reverse maneuvers.

causes.cause: Sensor covered by paint, ice, or mud

symptoms: Parking assist chimes constantly or not at all
System indicates object present when nothing is behind the vehicle
One or more sensors not responding

fixSteps.title: Clean sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Clean all rear bumper parking sensors with a damp cloth. Remove any mud, ice, or snow covering the sensor faces.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for damage
fixSteps.instruction: Check each sensor for physical damage, cracks, or displacement from bumper impacts.

fixSteps.title: Test individual sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Place your finger on each sensor while the system is active (reverse gear). You should feel a faint clicking vibration from a working sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the damaged sensor from behind the bumper and press in the replacement sensor. Match the color if the sensor is painted.

faq.q: Can I paint replacement parking sensors?
faq.a: Yes, but use very thin coats of paint. Thick paint can dampen the ultrasonic signal and cause the sensor to malfunction. Some suppliers offer pre-painted sensors in OEM colors.

faq.q: Why does my parking sensor beep when nothing is there?
faq.a: False alerts can be caused by dirt on the sensor face, water droplets, ice, or sensor misalignment. A faulty sensor can also generate ghost readings.

title: Backup Camera Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: The backup camera video circuit has a fault.
fullDescription:
Code B1610 indicates a fault in the backup (reverse) camera system. The backup camera provides a video feed to the infotainment display when reverse gear is selected. Federal regulations require backup cameras on all new vehicles sold in the US since 2018.

The camera is typically mounted in the tailgate handle, license plate light area, or rear bumper. Common failure modes include lens fogging from moisture intrusion, camera module failure, video cable damage, or connection issues at the head unit or camera end.

A non-functional backup camera reduces the driver's awareness of objects, children, and pedestrians behind the vehicle during reverse maneuvers. Drivers who have become accustomed to using the camera may be particularly affected.
safetyImpact: No rear video feed when reversing, reducing awareness of objects and pedestrians behind the vehicle.

causes.cause: Camera lens fogged or obscured by moisture
Video cable damage or loose connection

symptoms: No camera image when in reverse
Camera image is black, blurry, or distorted
Camera image freezes or has lines

fixSteps.title: Clean camera lens
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the backup camera lens with a soft cloth. Dirt, road film, and water can obscure the image.

fixSteps.title: Check connections
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the camera wiring connector at the tailgate (for tailgate-mounted cameras). Check for damage at the tailgate hinge area.

fixSteps.title: Test camera with monitor
fixSteps.instruction: If available, connect the camera to a separate test monitor to determine if the camera or the head unit is at fault.

fixSteps.title: Replace camera
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and replace the backup camera module. Reconnect all wiring and verify the image quality and guidelines on the display.

faq.q: Why is my backup camera image foggy?
faq.a: Moisture has likely entered the camera housing. The internal lens fogs up, making the image blurry. The camera housing seal has failed and the camera should be replaced.

faq.q: Can I install an aftermarket backup camera?
faq.a: Yes. Aftermarket backup cameras are available that can connect to existing displays or come with a separate monitor. Wireless options are also available for easier installation.

title: Power Trunk/Liftgate Motor Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: The power trunk or liftgate motor circuit has an electrical fault.
fullDescription:
Code B1700 indicates a fault in the power trunk lid or power liftgate motor circuit. Vehicles equipped with power trunks or power liftgates use one or two electric strut motors to automatically open and close the trunk or liftgate when commanded.

The system includes the strut motors, a latch mechanism, position sensors, and a pinch detection system (to prevent closing on objects or people). A fault in any of these components can trigger this code.

Power liftgate/trunk failures can leave the trunk unable to be opened or closed electrically. Most systems have a manual override or release to allow manual operation in case of motor failure.
safetyImpact: Trunk or liftgate may not close fully, potentially opening while driving. Manual operation remains available.
symptoms: Trunk/liftgate does not open or close automatically
Trunk/liftgate reverses during closing (pinch sensor false trigger)
Power strut makes noise but does not move trunk

fixSteps.title: Check for obstruction
fixSteps.instruction: Verify nothing is blocking the trunk/liftgate travel path. Even a small item in the weather strip can trigger the pinch sensor.

fixSteps.title: Reset system
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the battery for 5 minutes to reset the liftgate module. Some systems require a relearn procedure after reset.

fixSteps.title: Test strut motors
fixSteps.instruction: Check voltage at the strut motor connectors during operation. Test motors individually by applying direct power.

fixSteps.title: Replace strut or motor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty power strut or motor. Power struts are typically held by ball studs and can be replaced with basic hand tools.

faq.q: My power liftgate keeps reversing when trying to close. What is wrong?
faq.a: The anti-pinch sensor is detecting an obstruction. This can be a genuine obstruction, a misaligned weather strip, or a faulty pinch sensor strip along the liftgate edge.

faq.q: Can I manually open the trunk if the power system fails?
faq.a: Yes. Most power trunks and liftgates have a manual release inside the vehicle (typically a button or lever near the driver's seat) and some have a manual release inside the trunk.

title: Passenger Frontal Airbag Stage 1 Deployment Control
shortDescription: Malfunction in the passenger-side frontal airbag stage 1 deployment circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2001 indicates a fault in the passenger frontal stage 1 airbag deployment control circuit. The airbag control module (ACM) has detected an abnormal condition in the squib circuit that fires the first stage of the passenger frontal airbag.

The passenger frontal airbag is located in the dashboard in front of the passenger seat. The squib circuit must maintain specific resistance values to be considered operational. Any deviation from the expected resistance range will set this code and disable the passenger airbag.

This is a critical safety code. The passenger frontal airbag will not deploy in a collision while this code is active. Repair should be performed immediately and only by qualified technicians familiar with airbag safety procedures.
safetyImpact: Passenger frontal airbag will not deploy in a collision, significantly increasing risk of serious injury.

causes.cause: Open or shorted wiring in airbag circuit

symptoms: Passenger airbag OFF indicator may display
DTC stored in airbag control module

fixSteps.title: Scan SRS system
fixSteps.instruction: Use an SRS-capable scanner to read all airbag codes. Record freeze frame data for diagnosis.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the battery and wait 10+ minutes before any airbag work. Inspect the wiring from the ACM to the passenger airbag module for damage.

fixSteps.title: Check squib resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the squib circuit resistance at the airbag connector (battery disconnected, 10 min wait). Compare to specification (typically 2-4 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Replace airbag module
fixSteps.instruction: If the squib is out of specification, the airbag module must be replaced. This must be done with the battery disconnected and following all safety procedures.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with a passenger airbag code?
faq.a: The vehicle will drive normally, but the passenger airbag will not deploy in a crash. No one should ride in the passenger seat until this is repaired.

faq.q: Can I repair airbag wiring myself?
faq.a: Airbag system work is dangerous. The squib circuit can cause unintended deployment if improperly handled. Professional repair is strongly recommended.

title: Side Curtain Airbag Circuit Malfunction - Left
shortDescription: The left side curtain airbag deployment circuit has an electrical fault.
fullDescription:
Code B2010 indicates a fault in the left side curtain airbag circuit. Side curtain airbags are mounted in the roof rail above the side windows and deploy downward in a side impact or rollover to protect occupants' heads from contact with the side structure and intruding objects.

The side curtain airbag covers a larger area than the front airbag and typically protects both front and rear occupants on the affected side. It deploys from the headliner and fills with gas to create a curtain between the occupants and the side window/door.

This is a safety-critical code. The left side curtain airbag will not deploy in a side impact or rollover while this code is active. The airbag warning light will be illuminated.
safetyImpact: Left side curtain airbag will not deploy in a side impact or rollover, increasing head injury risk for left-side occupants.

causes.cause: Wiring damage in roof headliner area
Corroded connector in B-pillar or roof area

symptoms: DTC stored in airbag control module

fixSteps.title: Full SRS scan
fixSteps.instruction: Read all SRS codes with an SRS-capable scanner. Check for multiple curtain airbag codes which may indicate a common wiring issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect B-pillar wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery and wait 10 minutes. Remove B-pillar trim to inspect the curtain airbag wiring connector and harness.

fixSteps.title: Check squib circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the side curtain airbag squib resistance at the connector. Compare to specifications.

fixSteps.title: Replace curtain airbag
fixSteps.instruction: If the squib is faulty, the curtain airbag module must be replaced. This involves removing headliner trim and routing the new module along the roof rail.

faq.q: Where is the side curtain airbag located?
faq.a: Side curtain airbags are concealed in the roof headliner along the side of the vehicle, above the side windows. They deploy downward to cover the side windows.

faq.q: Can a previous accident have caused this code?
faq.a: Yes. If the vehicle was in a side impact that did not deploy the curtain airbag, the wiring or connector may have been damaged without being discovered.

title: Side Curtain Airbag Circuit Malfunction - Right
shortDescription: The right side curtain airbag deployment circuit has an electrical fault.
fullDescription:
Code B2015 indicates a fault in the right side curtain airbag circuit. This is the mirror counterpart of B2010, affecting the right side of the vehicle. The right side curtain airbag protects the front and rear right-side occupants during a right-side impact or rollover event.

The curtain airbag wiring runs through the headliner, A-pillar, B-pillar, and C-pillar areas. Any headliner removal, sunroof installation, or roof rack work can potentially damage the curtain airbag wiring.

As with all airbag codes, this is safety-critical. The right side curtain airbag will not deploy while this code is active.
safetyImpact: Right side curtain airbag will not deploy in a side impact or rollover, increasing head injury risk for right-side occupants.

causes.cause: Wiring damage from headliner or pillar work

fixSteps.title: Read SRS codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan the SRS module for all codes. Determine if only the right curtain airbag is affected or if multiple circuits have faults.

fixSteps.title: Inspect right pillar wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery and wait 10 minutes. Remove right side B-pillar and C-pillar trim to inspect wiring connectors.

fixSteps.title: Test squib resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure squib circuit resistance at the connector with battery disconnected.

fixSteps.title: Replace curtain airbag or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Repair damaged wiring or replace the curtain airbag module if the squib is faulty.

faq.q: Can aftermarket sunroof installation damage the curtain airbag?
faq.a: Yes. Aftermarket sunroof installation cuts into the roof structure near where the curtain airbag is routed. Improper installation can damage or disable the curtain airbag wiring.

faq.q: Will the curtain airbag deploy in a rollover?
faq.a: Yes. Modern curtain airbags are designed to deploy in both side impacts and rollover events. They use a rollover sensor to detect the vehicle tipping and deploy to protect occupants' heads.

title: Seat Side Airbag Circuit Malfunction - Driver
shortDescription: The driver seat-mounted side airbag deployment circuit has an electrical fault.
fullDescription:
Code B2050 indicates a fault in the driver seat-mounted side airbag (thorax airbag) circuit. This airbag is built into the outboard side of the driver seat backrest and deploys to protect the driver's torso in a side impact collision.

The seat side airbag deploys from the seat, not the door, which means it moves with the seat when the seat is adjusted. The wiring for this airbag runs through the seat frame and connects via a connector under the seat. This under-seat connector is a common failure point.

Aftermarket seat covers, seat heater installations, or objects stored beside the seat can interfere with the seat side airbag or damage its wiring.
safetyImpact: Driver side torso airbag will not deploy in a side collision, increasing risk of chest and abdominal injuries.

causes.cause: Aftermarket seat cover blocking deployment path

symptoms: SRS fault stored in airbag module
Side airbag deployment seam visible on seat

fixSteps.title: Inspect seat side
fixSteps.instruction: Check the outboard side of the driver seat for any aftermarket seat covers that may block the airbag deployment seam (marked with SRS or AIRBAG tag).

fixSteps.title: Check under-seat connector
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery and wait 10 minutes. Look under the driver seat for the yellow airbag connector. Ensure it is securely connected and not damaged.

fixSteps.title: Test circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Measure squib resistance at the connector. Check wiring continuity from the connector through the seat frame to the airbag module.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair damaged wiring or replace the seat side airbag module. This requires removing the seat cover from the affected area of the seat.

faq.q: Can seat covers cause this code?
faq.a: Yes. Seat covers can pinch or damage the airbag wiring at the seat frame. More importantly, non-airbag-compatible seat covers can block the deployment path, which may not set a code but will prevent proper deployment.

faq.q: Where exactly is the seat side airbag?
faq.a: It is located inside the outboard bolster of the seat backrest. Look for a tag that says SRS or AIRBAG on the side of the seat. The deployment seam is designed to tear open when the airbag inflates.

title: Airbag Control Module (ACM) Internal Fault
shortDescription: The airbag control module has detected an internal hardware or software fault.
fullDescription:
Code B2100 indicates the airbag control module (ACM) has detected an internal failure in its own circuitry. The ACM is the central processor for the entire supplemental restraint system, managing crash sensors, deployment logic, occupant detection, and communication with all airbag squibs.

An internal ACM fault is a critical condition that affects the entire airbag system. No airbags or pretensioners will deploy while this code is active. The ACM continuously monitors its internal processor, memory, impact sensors, and deployment drivers for correct operation.

ACM internal faults cannot typically be repaired. The module must be replaced and programmed to the vehicle. After ACM replacement, all crash sensor data is reset and the system must be verified.
safetyImpact: Entire airbag and supplemental restraint system is disabled. No airbags will deploy in any collision type.

causes.cause: ACM internal processor or memory failure

fixSteps.title: Verify code
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm the internal fault code with an SRS-capable scanner. Check for any other codes that might indicate an external cause (voltage issue, water damage).

fixSteps.title: Inspect ACM location
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the ACM (typically under the center console or under the driver/passenger seat). Check for water damage, corrosion, or physical damage.

fixSteps.title: Check power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify all ACM power supply and ground connections are clean and providing correct voltage. A poor connection can cause internal errors.

fixSteps.title: Replace ACM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the ACM with a new unit. The replacement ACM must be programmed to the vehicle VIN and configured for the vehicle's specific airbag configuration.

faq.q: Can a voltage spike damage the ACM?
faq.a: Yes. Jump-starting the vehicle incorrectly, a failing alternator producing voltage spikes, or welding on the vehicle without disconnecting the ACM can damage the sensitive internal electronics.

faq.q: Is the ACM the same as the SDM or AECM?
faq.a: Different manufacturers use different names: ACM (Airbag Control Module), SDM (Sensing and Diagnostic Module), AECM (Airbag Electronic Control Module), RCM (Restraint Control Module). They all perform the same function.

title: Climate Control Blower Motor Circuit Failure
shortDescription: Circuit fault in the HVAC blower motor control.
fullDescription:
Code B2103 is set when the body control module (BCM) or HVAC control module detects an abnormal electrical condition in the blower motor control circuit. This can be an open circuit, short to ground, short to battery voltage, or out-of-range command response from the blower speed controller.

The HVAC system may operate at one speed only, not at all, or intermittently. Because the code is a body/comfort fault it does not affect the engine, but cabin defogging and heating may be compromised, which is a safety concern in cold or humid climates.

Diagnosis involves verifying battery voltage and ground at the blower motor, commanding the blower through different speeds with a bidirectional scanner, and inspecting the blower motor resistor or power module for the most common failures.
safetyImpact: Reduced defrost and defog capability may impair driver visibility in humid or cold weather.

causes.cause: Failed blower motor resistor or power module
Seized or worn blower motor drawing excessive current
Open or shorted wiring between BCM and blower
Failed HVAC control head or BCM

symptoms: Blower stuck on one speed or inoperative
HVAC light on climate panel illuminated

fixSteps.title: Retrieve codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan body module for all B-codes and freeze-frame data.

fixSteps.title: Inspect blower resistor
fixSteps.instruction: Access the blower resistor under the passenger dash and inspect for heat damage or melted connector.

fixSteps.title: Test blower motor current
fixSteps.instruction: Command blower on high and measure amperage draw; excessive draw indicates a failing motor.

fixSteps.title: Replace failed component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace blower resistor, motor or harness as indicated, clear codes and verify operation at all speeds.

faq.q: Why does my blower only work on high?
faq.a: The most common cause is a failed blower motor resistor: high speed bypasses the resistor pack, so it continues to work even when other speeds fail.

faq.q: Can I drive the car with this code?
faq.a: Yes, the vehicle is safe to drive, but defrost and defog performance will be reduced.

title: Transponder Key Not Programmed
shortDescription: The ignition key transponder is not recognized by the immobilizer.
fullDescription:
Code B2139 indicates that the transponder chip embedded in the ignition key is communicating with the immobilizer antenna but has not been programmed into the vehicle's immobilizer module. The engine may start briefly and stall, or may not crank at all depending on the manufacturer strategy.

This commonly occurs after purchasing a replacement key that has not been coded, after a module replacement, or when a dealer-programmed key has been removed from the list. The code is stored in the immobilizer or body control module.

Resolution generally requires a scan tool capable of key coding or a visit to the dealer. Some vehicles allow owner-programmed keys using an existing working key; others require PIN and locksmith-level tools.
safetyImpact: Vehicle will not start; stranding risk.

causes.cause: New key not programmed to the immobilizer
Failed immobilizer antenna ring around ignition
BCM or immobilizer module requires reprogramming

symptoms: Engine cranks but will not start
Vehicle starts then stalls within 2 seconds

fixSteps.title: Verify key is correct
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm the key blank and transponder are the correct part number for this VIN.

fixSteps.title: Attempt owner programming
fixSteps.instruction: If applicable, follow the owner-programming sequence using a known-good key.

fixSteps.title: Program with scan tool
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool with immobilizer functions to add the key, requiring the security PIN.

fixSteps.title: Inspect antenna ring
fixSteps.instruction: If programming fails, test the immobilizer antenna around the ignition cylinder for continuity.

faq.q: Can I program the key myself?
faq.a: Some manufacturers allow owner programming if you already have two working keys. Most modern vehicles require a scan tool and the security PIN.

faq.q: Will a locksmith be cheaper than the dealer?
faq.a: Often yes — automotive locksmiths can cut and program keys on-site for 30-50% less than a dealer.

title: Non-Volatile Memory Not Programmed
shortDescription: Module NVM or VIN configuration has not been written.
fullDescription:
Code B2141 is set when a body module reports that its non-volatile memory has not been programmed with vehicle configuration data (typically VIN, options, or regional settings). This is most common after a module replacement where the installer did not complete the programming procedure.

Unprogrammed NVM can disable features such as remote keyless entry, automatic locks, or immobilizer functions and frequently causes a cluster of companion codes. The vehicle may still drive, but affected systems will not operate correctly.

Programming is performed with a factory or equivalent aftermarket scan tool connected to manufacturer service information. DIY-level OBD-II tools cannot perform this operation.
safetyImpact: Security and lighting features may be disabled; limited safety impact.

causes.cause: Corrupted NVM from low battery during coding

fixSteps.title: Verify module part number
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm the installed module matches the VIN and option package.

fixSteps.title: Check battery voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure system voltage is stable above 12.6V with charger attached during programming.

fixSteps.title: Perform NVM programming
fixSteps.instruction: Using factory-level scan tool, program VIN and configuration from OEM service data.

fixSteps.title: Verify all features
fixSteps.instruction: Cycle through locks, windows, remote functions and confirm B2141 does not return.

faq.q: Can I drive the car like this?
faq.a: Usually yes, but expect erratic behavior from affected systems until programming is complete.

faq.q: Why does a new module need programming?
faq.a: Modern modules ship blank so the same part can fit thousands of different vehicle configurations.

title: Security Lockout Active
shortDescription: Immobilizer has entered a timed lockout after failed start attempts.
fullDescription:
Code B2195 is stored when the immobilizer has detected multiple failed authentication attempts and entered a timed lockout. During lockout, the vehicle will not start even with a valid key. Typical lockouts last 10-30 minutes depending on the manufacturer.

The code can be triggered by a weak or damaged transponder, a failed antenna ring, or intentional tampering. Some vehicles will store B2195 after a dead battery event because the immobilizer boot sequence timed out.

Clearing typically only requires waiting out the lockout. Repeat occurrences point to an underlying key or antenna fault that should be diagnosed.
safetyImpact: Vehicle will not start; potential stranding.

fixSteps.title: Wait out lockout
fixSteps.instruction: Leave key in ON position for 10-20 minutes until security light stops flashing.

fixSteps.title: Retry with spare key
fixSteps.instruction: Attempt start with a different programmed key.

fixSteps.title: Scan for companion codes
fixSteps.instruction: Pull all B-codes; look for B2139 or B2141 that point to the root cause.

fixSteps.title: Repair underlying fault
fixSteps.instruction: Program new key, replace antenna ring, or repair wiring as indicated.

faq.q: How long does the lockout last?
faq.a: Most vehicles use a 10-minute lockout. Some BMW, Ford and GM vehicles use progressive lockouts up to 30 minutes.

faq.q: Can I bypass the lockout?
faq.a: No, there is no legitimate bypass. Any advertised bypass tool is typically either fraudulent or illegal.

title: Ignition Switch Run/Start Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Abnormal reading on the ignition switch run or start input.
fullDescription:
Code B2204 indicates the BCM has detected an invalid combination of signals on the ignition switch inputs — for example, both Run and Start high simultaneously, or neither signal present when cranking. The fault can prevent the engine from starting or cause the vehicle to remain energized after the key is removed.

Common causes include worn ignition switch contacts, a dropped key cylinder actuator, or wiring damage in the steering column. Push-button start vehicles can set this code when the start relay sticks or the brake switch fails.

Diagnosis requires back-probing the ignition switch connector while cycling the key, comparing to expected voltage patterns in the service manual.
safetyImpact: Intermittent no-start or failure to power down; possible stall risk.
symptoms: Vehicle stays powered after key removal
Multiple modules report loss of ignition

fixSteps.title: Retrieve codes
fixSteps.instruction: Capture freeze-frame data showing ignition state when fault set.

fixSteps.title: Inspect brake switch
fixSteps.instruction: Verify brake switch operation — push-button vehicles require it for start.

fixSteps.title: Back-probe switch
fixSteps.instruction: Measure Run and Start circuits through all key positions per service data.

fixSteps.title: Replace switch or relay
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ignition switch electrical portion or start relay based on findings.

faq.q: Why does my car stay on after I take the key out?
faq.a: The Run signal is stuck high because the ignition switch contacts failed in the closed position.

faq.q: Will this drain my battery?
faq.a: Yes, if the vehicle remains powered after key-off, battery drain of 3-5 amps is typical and will discharge the battery overnight.

title: Anti-Theft Alarm Triggered
shortDescription: Vehicle anti-theft system has been tripped.
fullDescription:
Code B2225 stores a history event showing that the factory anti-theft alarm was triggered. The BCM or security module records which sensor tripped the alarm — most often a door ajar, glass-break, tilt, or hood switch input while the alarm was armed.

Repeated false alarms typically point to a failing door jamb switch, shock sensor sensitivity set too high, or hood-switch corrosion. Leaving the code unresolved causes repeated battery drains and nuisance alarms in parking lots.

Diagnosis uses the scan tool's 'last alarm trigger' data PID to identify the offending input, followed by inspection of that sensor or switch.
safetyImpact: No driving safety impact; nuisance and battery drain.
symptoms: Remote won't arm or disarm consistently

fixSteps.title: Read trigger history
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool to read the last five alarm trigger sources.

fixSteps.title: Inspect suspect switch
fixSteps.instruction: Open and close the indicated door or hood; verify switch state on scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Clean or replace switch
fixSteps.instruction: Clean corrosion with contact cleaner or replace switch if intermittent.

fixSteps.title: Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Arm alarm and monitor overnight; confirm B2225 does not set again.

faq.q: Can I disable the alarm entirely?
faq.a: Most factory alarms can be disabled via scan-tool programming, but this also disables the immobilizer on some vehicles.

faq.q: Why does my alarm go off in the rain?
faq.a: Water intrusion into a jamb switch or hood pin switch is a common cause of rain-triggered alarms.

title: Class 2 / Security Data Circuit Performance
shortDescription: Security-related data bus messages are missing or corrupted.
fullDescription:
Code B2240 indicates the BCM or security module cannot reliably exchange messages with other modules on the Class 2 (GM) or comparable manufacturer bus. Security messages such as immobilizer authorization and theft-deterrent status cannot be validated.

The usual cause is a wiring fault on the bus, a failed module pulling the bus low, or a bad module connector. Once a module drops off the bus, the security system typically defaults to the safest state and may prevent starting.

Diagnosis uses an oscilloscope or bus monitor to observe Class 2 traffic while unplugging modules one at a time until the bus recovers.
safetyImpact: Possible no-start or loss of multiple body features.
symptoms: Multiple unrelated B and U codes

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Check every module for U-codes to map which can and cannot communicate.

fixSteps.title: Measure bus voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With key on, verify Class 2 bus rests near 0V and pulses to 7V briefly.

fixSteps.title: Isolate modules
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug suspect modules one at a time; note when communication returns.

fixSteps.title: Repair harness or module
fixSteps.instruction: Repair damaged wiring or replace failed module as identified.

faq.q: Can a weak battery cause this code?
faq.a: Yes, low voltage during cranking can corrupt bus messaging and set B2240 as a history code.

faq.q: Do I need to replace the BCM?
faq.a: Not usually — most B2240 faults are wiring or a single failed module, not the BCM itself.

title: Passenger Seat Belt Switch Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in the passenger seat belt buckle switch circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2350 indicates an open, shorted, or erratic signal from the passenger seat belt buckle switch. The body or airbag module uses this signal for the seat-belt reminder chime, for deployment logic on dual-stage airbags, and for occupant classification when tied to the OCS.

Common failures include drinks spilled into the buckle, the switch contacts wearing out, or a broken wire where the buckle harness flexes. The belt mechanically works, but the electrical indication does not.

Diagnosis measures the resistance across the belt switch in both latched and unlatched states against the service-manual specification.
safetyImpact: May disable seat belt reminder and alter airbag deployment staging.
symptoms: Passenger belt chime does not activate

fixSteps.title: Capture freeze frame
fixSteps.instruction: Record whether fault occurred belt-latched or unlatched.

fixSteps.title: Inspect buckle
fixSteps.instruction: Look inside buckle for contamination; clean with contact cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Test switch resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect seat harness and measure switch in both states.

fixSteps.title: Replace buckle
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the seat belt buckle assembly if switch fails specification.

faq.q: Is the belt itself unsafe?
faq.a: No, the webbing and latch still function mechanically. Only the electrical indication is affected.

faq.q: Can I just ignore this code?
faq.a: Not recommended — on vehicles with dual-stage airbags and OCS, the code can change deployment behavior in a crash.

title: Driver Seat Belt Pretensioner Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Fault detected in the driver seat belt pretensioner squib circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2362 indicates the airbag control module has detected an out-of-range resistance, open, or short in the driver seat belt pretensioner pyrotechnic circuit. Pretensioners fire during a collision to remove slack from the belt before airbag deployment, and a circuit fault means this safety device may not fire.

Common causes include corroded connectors under the seat, a crushed harness from sliding the seat fully forward, or a defective pretensioner unit. The airbag warning light will be on whenever B2362 is active.

Diagnosis uses shorting bars and SRS-safe multimeter leads. Work must be done with the battery disconnected for at least 10 minutes to safely discharge reserve capacitors.
safetyImpact: Pretensioner may not fire in a collision, increasing injury risk.

fixSteps.title: Disconnect battery
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery and wait 10 minutes before any SRS work.

fixSteps.title: Inspect under-seat connector
fixSteps.instruction: Slide seat fully forward and back, inspect yellow SRS connector for damage.

fixSteps.title: Test squib resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Using SRS-safe shorting adapter and meter, verify squib resistance matches spec (1-5 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Replace pretensioner or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace pretensioner assembly or repair harness, reconnect and clear codes.

faq.q: Is the pretensioner reusable after deployment?
faq.a: No — once fired, the pyrotechnic charge is spent and the entire assembly must be replaced.

faq.q: Can I work on SRS components myself?
faq.a: Only with proper training and SRS-rated tools. Accidental deployment can cause serious injury.

title: Passenger Airbag Indicator Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Fault in the PASSENGER AIRBAG OFF indicator lamp circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2421 indicates a fault in the passenger-airbag ON/OFF indicator lamp circuit, typically mounted near the center of the dashboard. The airbag module drives this lamp based on the Occupant Classification System (OCS) to tell occupants whether the passenger airbag is armed.

A circuit fault means the indicator may be stuck on, stuck off, or not match the actual airbag state. This is a reportable defect in many jurisdictions because occupants rely on the indicator to reposition or secure child seats correctly.

Diagnosis checks the bulb (if used), the driver transistor in the airbag module, and the wiring between the module and the indicator.
safetyImpact: Driver may be misinformed about passenger airbag state; child-seat placement risk.

causes.cause: Burned out indicator LED or bulb

symptoms: Passenger airbag indicator does not illuminate
Indicator stuck ON even with adult occupant

fixSteps.title: Scan codes
fixSteps.instruction: Pull SRS codes; look for OCS-related companions.

fixSteps.title: Bulb / indicator check
fixSteps.instruction: Access indicator; verify continuity or LED operation with bench power.

fixSteps.title: Test circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe the indicator driver circuit and compare to service spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace indicator or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace indicator module or repair harness; clear codes and verify operation.

faq.q: Is the passenger airbag disabled when this code is active?
faq.a: It depends on the strategy — some vehicles disable the airbag as a precaution, others only disable the indicator.

faq.q: Do I need OCS recalibration after the repair?
faq.a: If the seat was removed or the OCS was disturbed, yes — a mat calibration is required.

title: Information Display Module Configuration Failure
shortDescription: Information display module failed to load configuration.
fullDescription:
Code B2477 indicates the information display module (IPC or center display) failed to retrieve or validate its configuration data at startup. The module may boot into a default or limited-feature mode, with incorrect gauges, missing menu options, or a blank center display.

This typically occurs after a module replacement without proper configuration, after a flash programming interruption, or after a battery disconnect during bootup. The code is usually cleared by performing a proper module configuration with a scan tool.

Running the vehicle for extended periods in this state is safe, but odometer, speedometer, and warning messages may not be accurate.
safetyImpact: Incorrect gauge or warning information may mislead driver.
symptoms: Display shows default or English-only menus

fixSteps.title: Check battery
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm battery at 12.6V+ and stable before attempting programming.

fixSteps.title: Perform configuration
fixSteps.instruction: Using factory scan tool, load configuration data from OEM database.

fixSteps.title: Validate features
fixSteps.instruction: Cycle ignition, verify gauges and menus restore to full functionality.

fixSteps.title: Retest after drive cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Drive and confirm B2477 does not return.

faq.q: Will this damage my car?
faq.a: No, but gauges may be wrong. Use a GPS speed app until configured.

faq.q: Can AutoZone do this programming?
faq.a: Usually not — full configuration typically requires dealer-level tools.

title: Display Dimmer / Brightness Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in the display brightness control circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2490 is stored when the BCM or display module detects an out-of-range voltage on the brightness/dimmer control circuit. The dimmer input normally varies with the dash illumination wheel or daylight sensor; an open, short to ground, or short to voltage triggers the code.

Symptoms range from display stuck at full brightness, stuck off, or flickering. On some vehicles, the radio and HVAC backlighting are shared with this circuit and all go dark together.

Diagnosis measures the dimmer voltage at the illumination wheel through its full travel and verifies the ambient light sensor when equipped.
safetyImpact: Overly bright display at night may reduce forward visibility.
symptoms: Display brightness doesn't respond to dimmer
Display stuck at full or minimum brightness

fixSteps.title: Actuate dimmer wheel
fixSteps.instruction: Watch scan-tool dimmer PID while turning the wheel.

fixSteps.title: Test ambient sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Cover/uncover the dash sensor and watch PID change; replace if no response.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect illumination harness behind dash for pinched wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace dimmer or sensor based on test results.

faq.q: Can I drive with this code?
faq.a: Yes, though a too-bright display can be distracting at night.

faq.q: Why does my radio go dark too?
faq.a: Many vehicles share one illumination circuit across radio, HVAC, and cluster.

title: Occupant Classification System Fault
shortDescription: OCS cannot reliably determine passenger seat occupant status.
fullDescription:
Code B2580 indicates the Occupant Classification System (OCS) has encountered a fault. The OCS uses pressure sensors or strain gauges in the passenger seat to decide whether the seat is empty, holds a child seat, or holds an adult. This determines whether the passenger airbag is armed and whether dual-stage deployment fires both stages.

Faults commonly result from a disturbed sensor mat (seat removed and reinstalled without recalibration), damaged wiring from items kicked under the seat, or internal sensor failure. The PASSENGER AIRBAG OFF indicator may become unreliable.

Diagnosis requires a scan tool capable of OCS zero-point recalibration. If recalibration fails, the mat or sensor assembly typically must be replaced.
safetyImpact: Passenger airbag may fire on a child or fail to fire for an adult.
symptoms: Airbag stays off with adult occupant

fixSteps.title: Empty seat
fixSteps.instruction: Remove all items from passenger seat and floor area.

fixSteps.title: Perform zero calibration
fixSteps.instruction: Using factory scan tool, run OCS zero-point reset with empty seat.

fixSteps.title: Inspect under-seat harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check for damaged wiring or crushed clips beneath seat.

fixSteps.title: Replace seat sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If calibration fails, replace OCS mat or complete seat cushion assembly.

faq.q: Why is the OCS mat so expensive?
faq.a: OCS mats are safety-critical and sold with the entire seat cushion on many vehicles.

faq.q: Will a child seat confuse the OCS?
faq.a: Properly installed LATCH child seats are recognized. A heavy adult-style booster may trigger the airbag to arm.

title: Rear Defroster Relay Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Fault in the rear window defroster relay control circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2603 indicates the BCM has detected an open, short, or abnormal feedback on the rear window defroster relay control circuit. The defroster uses high current to heat the rear glass grid, which is controlled through a relay driven by the BCM.

Failures include a stuck relay, open coil, or failed BCM driver. A failed relay may cause the defroster to operate continuously, draining battery and potentially damaging the rear glass grid, or not operate at all, impairing visibility.

Diagnosis involves commanding the relay via scan tool and verifying the control side voltage and the load side current.
safetyImpact: Reduced rear visibility in cold/humid conditions.

causes.cause: Open defroster grid or feed wire

symptoms: Defroster button LED does not illuminate

fixSteps.title: Command relay
fixSteps.instruction: Activate defroster relay with scan tool bidirectional control.

fixSteps.title: Swap relay
fixSteps.instruction: Swap defroster relay with a known-good identical relay.

fixSteps.title: Test grid continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Measure grid with meter — 3-15 ohms is typical.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring or BCM
fixSteps.instruction: If command side is dead, repair harness or reprogram/replace BCM.

faq.q: Why did my relay stick?
faq.a: Most defroster relays fail due to arcing across the high-current contacts over years of use.

faq.q: Can I bypass the relay?
faq.a: Not recommended — the relay protects the BCM from the ~30A defroster load.

title: Warning Chime Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Fault in the audible warning chime output circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2700 indicates the BCM has detected an abnormal condition on the warning chime driver circuit. The chime alerts the driver to key-in-ignition, door-ajar, seat-belt, and turn-signal states.

Faults include a failed chime speaker, shorted wiring, or a BCM output driver failure. Symptoms include missing chimes entirely, chimes that play continuously, or chimes that sound at the wrong time (e.g., beeping while driving).

Diagnosis commands the chime with a scan tool and verifies output drive at both the BCM and the chime module.
safetyImpact: Driver may miss audible alerts for door ajar, seatbelt, lights on, etc.

fixSteps.title: Command chime
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool to activate chime manually.

fixSteps.title: Inspect chime module
fixSteps.instruction: Locate chime speaker (often behind dash) and verify power/ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace chime
fixSteps.instruction: If power is present but no sound, replace chime speaker.

fixSteps.title: BCM reflash
fixSteps.instruction: If drivers are inactive, have BCM reflashed with latest calibration.

faq.q: Is this safety-critical?
faq.a: Not by itself, but missing chimes can let you leave lights on or drive with a door ajar.

faq.q: Can I replace just the speaker?
faq.a: Usually yes — the chime is a small piezo speaker under the dash.

title: Engine Immobilizer System Performance
shortDescription: Performance fault in the immobilizer / anti-theft system.
fullDescription:
Code B2799 is a generic performance fault for the engine immobilizer. The ECM expected an authorization message from the immobilizer module but did not receive one, or the message was invalid. The engine will typically crank briefly and stall, or not start at all.

Common causes include communication loss on the CAN/Class-2 bus between the ECM and immobilizer, a damaged antenna ring around the ignition cylinder, or a weak transponder in the key. Aftermarket remote starters can also interfere with immobilizer timing.

Diagnosis starts with scanning all modules for U-codes to confirm bus health, then verifying the antenna and key transponder are functional.
safetyImpact: Vehicle will not start; potential stranding.

fixSteps.title: Try spare key
fixSteps.instruction: Attempt start with original second key to isolate transponder.

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Check every module for U-codes.

fixSteps.title: Inspect antenna ring
fixSteps.instruction: Remove steering column cover and check antenna connector.

fixSteps.title: Program key or repair antenna
fixSteps.instruction: Reprogram key or replace antenna assembly, clear code and retest.

faq.q: Do aftermarket remote starts cause this?
faq.a: Yes — poorly installed bypass modules are a leading cause of B2799.

faq.q: Can a dead key battery cause this?
faq.a: Only for proximity/push-button keys; traditional transponder keys don't have a battery.

title: Radio Security Code Required
shortDescription: Radio locked awaiting anti-theft code entry.
fullDescription:
Code B2900 is set when the factory radio has entered anti-theft lockout and is awaiting the correct security code. This typically happens after a battery disconnect on vehicles whose radios store a theft-deterrent code in volatile memory.

No vehicle function is affected other than the radio itself. The code is cleared by entering the correct 4- or 5-digit anti-theft code via the radio preset buttons. The code is printed in the owner's manual card, stored in dealer records, or may be retrieved from the radio serial number via manufacturer lookup.

Modern vehicles with VIN-linked radios do not require customer code entry and will not set B2900.
safetyImpact: No safety impact.
symptoms: Radio display shows CODE or LOCKED

fixSteps.title: Locate security code
fixSteps.instruction: Check owner's manual glovebox card or purchase record.

fixSteps.title: Retrieve from dealer
fixSteps.instruction: If code is lost, dealer can look up using VIN and radio serial.

fixSteps.title: Enter code
fixSteps.instruction: Turn radio on and enter code using preset buttons as described in owner's manual.

fixSteps.title: Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm radio exits lockout and function is restored; clear code with scan tool.

faq.q: What if I enter the wrong code too many times?
faq.a: Most radios lock out after 3-10 wrong attempts and require a waiting period or dealer-only unlock.

faq.q: Is this covered under warranty?
faq.a: Usually no — code lookup is typically a chargeable dealer service.

title: Theft Deterrent Module Internal Fault
shortDescription: The theft deterrent module reports an internal self-test failure.
fullDescription:
Code B2935 indicates the theft deterrent module (or equivalent immobilizer module) has failed its internal self-test. The module is unable to perform authorization, and as a safe default the engine may not be allowed to start.

Causes include internal memory corruption (often after a voltage spike), a failed microcontroller, or damaged circuit board from moisture intrusion. The module is typically not serviceable and must be replaced and then programmed to the VIN.

Because this module authorizes starting, replacement must be done carefully and programming performed before the vehicle is driven.
safetyImpact: Vehicle will not start; potential stranding.

fixSteps.title: Verify module power
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm 12V and ground present at module connector.

fixSteps.title: Attempt reflash
fixSteps.instruction: Some B2935 faults respond to a factory reflash before replacement.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Install new or rebuilt theft deterrent module.

fixSteps.title: Program to VIN
fixSteps.instruction: Perform VIN programming and key learn; verify start.

faq.q: Can I transplant a used module?
faq.a: Rarely — modules are usually VIN-locked and must be virgin or dealer-reprogrammed.

faq.q: Will a reflash fix it?
faq.a: Sometimes — about 1 in 4 B2935 cases are corrected by reflash without module replacement.

title: Gateway Module Communication Fault
shortDescription: Central gateway module lost communication with one or more sub-networks.
fullDescription:
Code B3055 is stored when the central gateway module — the device that bridges the vehicle's multiple CAN buses — loses or cannot validate traffic on one of its sub-networks. Modern vehicles have 4-8 CAN buses (powertrain, chassis, body, infotainment, etc.) that the gateway must interconnect.

When the gateway faults, one area of the car typically goes dark — all body modules, or all chassis modules, or all infotainment. A U-code storm across many modules is typical.

Diagnosis uses a scan tool connected to each sub-network to identify which bus is offline, followed by inspection of the gateway connectors and terminations.
safetyImpact: Loss of multiple vehicle systems; possible limp mode.
symptoms: Scan tool cannot reach some modules

fixSteps.title: Map responding modules
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules; note which respond and which do not to identify affected bus.

fixSteps.title: Measure bus terminations
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN-H / CAN-L resistance on each bus (60 ohms nominal).

fixSteps.title: Isolate modules
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect suspect modules one at a time until bus returns.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace gateway
fixSteps.instruction: Repair harness or replace gateway module; program if replaced.

faq.q: What is a gateway module?
faq.a: A networking module that bridges multiple CAN buses so messages can move between powertrain, body, chassis, and infotainment domains.

faq.q: Can a low battery cause B3055?
faq.a: Yes — marginal battery voltage can corrupt gateway RAM and produce intermittent B3055 codes.

title: Climate Mode Door Actuator Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in the HVAC mode door actuator feedback circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2105 indicates the HVAC control head has detected an abnormal feedback signal from the mode door actuator, which directs airflow between panel, floor, and defrost vents. The actuator has an internal potentiometer providing position feedback.

Common failures include a broken actuator gear, a dead motor winding, or a failed feedback potentiometer. The airflow direction may be stuck at one outlet, click audibly behind the dash, or not move at all.

Diagnosis commands the actuator through its full travel with the scan tool while watching the feedback PID for smooth, linear change.
safetyImpact: Loss of defrost routing can reduce visibility in winter.
symptoms: Vent airflow stuck at one outlet
Defrost only or floor only output

fixSteps.title: Command actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Activate actuator through scan tool bidirectional control.

fixSteps.title: Listen for motor
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for movement; whir without travel indicates broken gear.

fixSteps.title: Test motor power
fixSteps.instruction: Verify command voltage reversing at motor terminals.

fixSteps.title: Replace actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Replace mode door actuator (usually accessible under dash).

faq.q: Why is airflow stuck on defrost?
faq.a: Many vehicles default to defrost as a fail-safe when the mode door actuator fails, so airflow is directed to the windshield.

faq.q: Can I replace just the gear?
faq.a: Aftermarket repair gears exist for some models, but most DIYers replace the complete actuator.

title: Climate Temperature Blend Actuator Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in the HVAC temperature blend door actuator circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2104 indicates the HVAC control head detected an abnormal condition on the temperature blend door actuator, which controls how much airflow passes through the heater core versus bypassing it. A failure causes the cabin to stuck at one temperature — hot or cold regardless of setting.

Common failures match the mode door actuator: broken plastic gears, dead motor windings, or failed feedback pots. On dual-zone systems, only one side may be affected.

Diagnosis commands the blend actuator from full cold to full hot and watches the feedback signal sweep smoothly.
safetyImpact: Overheating or inability to defrost can reduce comfort and visibility.

fixSteps.title: Command actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Sweep actuator via scan tool and watch PID.

fixSteps.title: Check feedback
fixSteps.instruction: Graph the feedback signal for dropouts indicating bad pot.

fixSteps.title: Access actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Remove glove box or lower dash panel to reach actuator.

fixSteps.title: Replace actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Install new actuator, recalibrate via scan tool.

faq.q: Why does my left side blow cold while right side is warm?
faq.a: Dual-zone systems have two blend actuators; one has failed.

faq.q: Do I need to recalibrate the actuator?
faq.a: Most vehicles auto-calibrate on the next key-on cycle, but some require a scan-tool reset.

title: Memory Seat Position Feedback Fault
shortDescription: Fault in the memory seat position sensor feedback.
fullDescription:
Code B2160 indicates the memory seat module has lost or received abnormal feedback from one of the seat position sensors (typically slide, recline, tilt, or height). The memory function will not operate until valid feedback is restored.

Common causes include a damaged sensor, pinched wiring under the seat, or a failed seat motor position encoder. Items rolling under the seat frequently crush harnesses and cause this code.

Diagnosis uses the scan tool to view each position sensor PID while manually moving the seat, looking for dropouts or stuck values.
safetyImpact: No safety impact.

fixSteps.title: Move seat
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool to watch all seat PIDs while moving seat manually.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Slide seat fully forward and back, inspect wiring under seat.

fixSteps.title: Test sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure sensor resistance or voltage at connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed position sensor and recalibrate memory positions.

faq.q: Can I still move the seat manually?
faq.a: Yes, manual controls still work — only the memory function is affected.

faq.q: Will this drain my battery?
faq.a: Usually not, but a stuck motor drawing current can drain the battery over days.

title: Door Lock Actuator Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Fault in a power door lock actuator circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2115 indicates the BCM has detected an open or short on one of the door lock actuator drive circuits. The specific door is usually identified by a sub-code. A failed actuator will not lock or unlock from the switch or remote.

Common causes include a seized or burned-out actuator motor, a broken wire in the door jamb grommet (from years of door opening flex), or corrosion in the actuator connector. The remainder of the door locks will still operate.

Diagnosis commands the specific actuator with a scan tool and measures current draw to distinguish between wiring faults and a seized motor.
safetyImpact: Affected door may not lock; security concern.
symptoms: One door won't lock or unlock electrically

fixSteps.title: Command lock
fixSteps.instruction: Activate specific actuator via scan tool and listen.

fixSteps.title: Flex jamb harness
fixSteps.instruction: Open/close door while watching scan tool for bus dropouts.

fixSteps.title: Inspect jamb wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Peel back jamb rubber boot and inspect for broken wires.

fixSteps.title: Replace actuator or splice wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Repair broken jamb wires or replace actuator through door skin.

faq.q: Why do door jamb wires break?
faq.a: Repeated flexing (thousands of cycles) work-hardens the copper until it cracks internally.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive?
faq.a: Yes, but manually verify the affected door is locked.

title: Parking Assist Sensor Fault
shortDescription: Fault detected in a park-assist ultrasonic sensor.
fullDescription:
Code B2535 indicates the park-assist module has detected an out-of-range or non-responding ultrasonic sensor in the front or rear bumper. A failed sensor typically disables the entire parking-assist system and illuminates a warning on the cluster.

Common causes are road-debris impact, bumper paintwork covering the sensor face, heavy mud, or a damaged harness from a bumper scrape. Sensors are located behind small circular cutouts on the bumper.

Diagnosis uses the scan tool to identify the specific failed sensor, then tapping each sensor to verify internal function.
safetyImpact: Parking assist unavailable; no impact on driving.

causes.cause: Mud or snow packed on sensor

fixSteps.title: Identify failed sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Scan to read which sensor(s) are reporting.

fixSteps.title: Clean sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Wash bumper and wipe sensor faces; retest.

fixSteps.title: Tap test
fixSteps.instruction: With key on, tap each sensor and listen for ultrasonic click.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove bumper or access through wheel arch to replace failed sensor.

faq.q: Can paint on the sensor cause this?
faq.a: Yes — aftermarket bumper paint over 50 microns thick blocks ultrasonic sensors.

faq.q: Do I need to code a new sensor?
faq.a: Most vehicles auto-learn replacement sensors; some European cars require coding.

title: Sunroof Motor Circuit Fault
shortDescription: Fault in the sunroof motor control or position circuit.
fullDescription:
Code B2755 indicates the BCM or roof control module has detected an open, short, or out-of-range position feedback from the sunroof motor. The sunroof may be stuck in one position, creep slowly, or fail pinch protection requiring a manual reset.

Common causes include a seized sunroof track (needs lubrication), a burned-out motor, or a failed hall-effect encoder on the motor shaft. Overheating due to a binding track will cause the motor to set this code after excessive current draw.

Diagnosis cycles the sunroof under command and monitors position feedback and current draw.
safetyImpact: Water intrusion if stuck open.

fixSteps.title: Lubricate track
fixSteps.instruction: Clean and lubricate the sunroof rails with approved grease.

fixSteps.title: Perform reset
fixSteps.instruction: Follow owner's manual sunroof reinitialization procedure.

fixSteps.title: Command motor
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool to command motor and watch current.

fixSteps.title: Replace motor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace sunroof motor assembly if seized or encoder failed.

faq.q: Do I need to reset after a dead battery?
faq.a: Most sunroofs require reinitialization after battery disconnect — press and hold close for 20 seconds.

faq.q: Why does the sunroof stop halfway?
faq.a: The motor is detecting excess current (a binding track) and stopping for safety.
